title: Probing PbTe-Pb nanowire devices with radio-frequency reflectometry
authors: Xin-Yi Tang, Lin Li, Zezhou Xia, Jierong Huo, Wenyu Song, Lining Yang, Zonglin Li, Jiaye Xu, Peilin Li, Runan Shang, Qi-Kun Xue, Ke He, Hao Zhang
abstract: We report the implementation of radio-frequency (rf) reflectometry on selective-area-grown PbTe-Pb nanowire devices on a CdTe substrate. These nanowires were predicted to host Majorana zero modes. We demonstrate the compatibility of the rf technique, both resistive and capacitive sensing, with these nanowires. The effect of dielectric loss from the CdTe substrate is also discussed. The feasibility of rf reflectometry is also verified at finite magnetic fields where zero-energy modes occur. Our results enable the fast control of PbTe quantum devices, paving the way for its application in topological quantum computation.
summary: This work demonstrates the feasibility of using radio-frequency (rf) reflectometry to rapidly probe the quantum conductance of PbTe-Pb nanowires designed to host Majorana zero modes. By measuring the reflected rf signal, the authors show a direct, fast correlation between the rf signal and the device's quantum conductance. This technique significantly accelerates characterization compared to traditional DC measurements, paving the way for topological quantum computation.
main_problem: The primary goal is to demonstrate a fast, non-invasive readout technique for quantum states in superconducting nanowires predicted to host Majorana zero modes.
main_result: Radio-frequency (rf) reflectometry proves to be a viable, order-of-magnitude faster method than DC transport for probing the quantum conductance of PbTe-Pb nanowires.
method: The technique involves using a Vector Network Analyzer (VNA) to measure the reflection coefficient ($S_{21}$) of the nanowire circuit as a function of gate voltage and frequency.
model_or_system: The experiment uses selective-area-grown PbTe-Pb nanowires on a CdTe substrate, which are engineered to host Majorana zero modes. The circuit is modeled as an LCR network, allowing the rf signal to probe the device's quantum conductance.
key_observables: The measured observables include the reflection coefficient ($|S_{21}|$) vs. gate voltage and frequency, the device conductance ($G$), and the derived relationship between the rf signal voltage ($V_{rf}$) and $G$.
important_parameters: Key parameters include the gate voltage ($V_{SG}$), rf frequency ($f$), and the magnetic field ($B$), which must be compatible with the zero-energy mode signatures.
main_assumptions: The analysis makes approximations regarding the circuit model, such as neglecting the bias-tee region for resonance analysis, and attributing small resonance dips to parasitic impedance mismatch.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the rf-readout schematic, gate-tunable reflectometry results showing $|S_{21}|$ vs $V_{SG}$ and $f$, comparisons between rf and dc probing speeds, and measurements demonstrating compatibility with magnetic fields.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the rf-readout system and its compatibility with the nanowire structure. It then presents data showing the gate-tunable response, followed by comparisons to DC measurements and studies of the system's response under applied magnetic fields and capacitive coupling.
why_interesting: The development of fast, high-bandwidth readout techniques like rf reflectometry is crucial for characterizing fragile quantum states, such as those associated with Majorana zero modes, which are central to topological quantum computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. rf-readout system for PbTe nanowires. (a) Schematic of fridge wiring. (b) Circuit diagram of the device PCB. The rf signal is injected from the ‘rf’ port. An off-chip bias-tee design enables simultaneous transport measurement through ‘dc-in’ and ‘dc-out’ ports. (c) An optical image of the device PCB, including the device chip (upper right) and a sapphire substrate carrying superconducting spiral inductors and bias-tees (the middle void). (d) False-colored SEM of the PbTe-Pb NS device. TG and SG refer to tunnel gate and super gate, respectively. (e) Simplified schematic of the device and circuit. For clarity, CdTe substrate/capping layer, PbEuTe buffer/spacing layer, gate...

FIG. 2. Gate-tunable reflectometry. (a) |S21| as a function of VSG and the rf frequency f. (b) Line cuts at several gate voltages (marked in (a)). (c) Transport measurement (upper) and the rf signal (lower). f is fixed at 241.1 MHz. (d) Vrf vs G. The red curve is the fitting.

FIG. 3. Comparison of rf and dc probing of the PbTe-Pb nanowire. (a) dc conductance map as a function of V and VSG. (b) rf measurement under the same gate and bias setting. (c) Line cuts from (a) (upper, black) and (b) (lower) at VSG = −3.22 V (marked as orange bars). The red curve in the upper panel is the rf signal converted into G. (d) Conductance (Grf) map converted from the rf signal in (b).

FIG. 4. Magnetic field compatibility. (a) The resonator re- sponse at different By’s (out-of-plane). VSG is fixed at −4.0 V. (b) The reflectometry signal as a function VSG at By of 0.0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 T, respectively. (c) rf signal (Vrf) vs G obtained at different fields.

FIG. 5. Resistive and capacitive sensing of PbTe field effect devices. (a) SEM of a PbTe device using the resistive readout. (b) |S21| as a function of VG and the rf frequency. (c) Line cuts at several gate voltages marked in (b). (d) Simulated cavity response for different device conductance. (e) SEM of a PbTe device using capacitive readout. (f) |S21| as a function of VG and the rf frequency. (g) Two line cuts from (f) at VG = 0 V and 2 V, respectively. (h) Conductance of this device as a function of VG. The magnetic field was set zero during the measurement of both devices.
